blocks.body:
• Personal / Object Pronouns — I/me, she/her, they/them.
  • These are the basic subject and object forms.
  • After them, move to possessive and reflexive forms.
blocks.heading: Personal and object pronouns

blocks.body:
• Possessive forms — my / mine, your / yours.
  • One form goes before a noun, the other replaces it.
  • This also prepares you for one / ones.
blocks.heading: Possessive forms

blocks.body:
• Reflexive Pronouns — myself, yourself, themselves.
  • They sit next to indefinite, relative, reciprocal pronouns and each / every / all.
  • Together they help you avoid repetition.
blocks.heading: Reflexive and related pronouns

blocks.body:
TopicLevelDescription
PronounsA1–A2Main pronoun groups
Personal / Object PronounsA1Subject and object forms
Possessive Adjectives vs PronounsA1–A2my / mine, your / yours
Reflexive PronounsA2–B1myself, yourself, themselves
Indefinite PronounsA2–B1someone, anyone, nobody
Relative PronounsB1who, which, that, whose
Each / Every / AllA2–B1each, every, all
Reciprocal PronounsA2–B1each other, one another
One / OnesA2Replacing a noun
blocks.heading: Topics in This Section

blocks.body:
• This / that / these / those show distance or closeness.
  • They help you point to people and things more clearly.
blocks.heading: Demonstratives

blocks.body:
🧠

Start with the overview, then move to personal, object, and possessive forms.

Study reflexive, indefinite, and relative pronouns as separate steps.

Each / Every / All, Reciprocal Pronouns and One / Ones close common confusion points.
blocks.heading: Study tips
